_comment: Mobile DAST findings.json — written by Claude at end of Phase 6, consumed by generate_report.py
_screenshot_naming: Name screenshots <finding-id>_<description>.png (e.g. F01_shared_prefs.png) so generate_report.py auto-attaches them.

findings.title: Sensitive Data Stored in Cleartext SharedPreferences
findings.mastgTest: MASTG-TEST-0001 — Testing Local Storage for Sensitive Data
findings.cwe: CWE-312 — Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
findings.description: After a successful login the application writes the session bearer token and the authenticated user's email address in plaintext to the Android SharedPreferences store (com.ohlq.app.dev_preferences.xml). Any process with READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE or a root shell can trivially read these values, enabling full account takeover without knowledge of the user's password.

evidence.title: Frida hook — token written at runtime
Source reference (jadx)

findings.remediation: Replace SharedPreferences with EncryptedSharedPreferences (AndroidX Security Crypto) backed by an Android KeyStore key. Reference: https://developer.android.com/topic/security/data

findings.title: Missing Certificate Pinning
findings.description: All HTTPS traffic between the application and its backend API is visible in plaintext through a standard mitmproxy interception (no bypass required). The application does not implement certificate pinning, allowing a network-level attacker to perform a MitM attack and intercept or tamper with authenticated requests including session tokens and personal data.

evidence.title: mitmproxy capturing authenticated request without SSL bypass
evidence.output:
GET /api/v1/user/profile HTTP/2.0
Host: api.ohlq.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJI...[REDACTED]
Content-Type: application/json

HTTP/2.0 200 OK
{"email":"[REDACTED:EMAIL]","name":"Test User",...}

findings.remediation: Implement certificate pinning via OkHttp's CertificatePinner or a network_security_config.xml <pin-set> block. Use public-key pins (SPKI), not certificate pins, to survive certificate renewals. Reference: https://owasp.org/www-community/controls/Certificate_and_Public_Key_Pinning

findings.title: Hardcoded API Keys in Application Bundle
findings.description: The application bundle contains hardcoded API keys for Braze (push notifications), Firebase (analytics), and Google Maps. These keys are embedded in the compiled APK and are trivially extractable with standard reverse-engineering tools. An attacker can use these keys to send unauthorized push notifications, access analytics data, or incur billing charges.
findings.remediation: Rotate all exposed keys immediately. Store secrets server-side and serve them to authenticated clients via a secure API endpoint at runtime. Use Google Cloud Secret Manager or equivalent. Never embed secrets in the APK.

findings.title: Application Screen Exposed in Recent Apps (Missing FLAG_SECURE)
findings.mastgTest: MASTG-TEST-0006 — Testing for Sensitive Data in the Keyboard Cache
findings.cwe: CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
findings.description: The application does not set FLAG_SECURE on its windows. Sensitive screens (login, account details, checkout) are visible as thumbnails in the Android Recent Apps switcher. On a shared or stolen device, another user can view these thumbnails without unlocking the application.

evidence.title: FLAG_SECURE absent in window dump
evidence.command: adb -s 127.0.0.1:6555 shell dumpsys window windows | grep -A 5 'com.ohlq.app.dev'
evidence.output:
Window{3a4b5c6 u0 com.ohlq.app.dev/com.ohlq.MainActivity}:
  mAttrs: flags=0x01810100 (no FLAG_SECURE / 0x00002000 bit absent)
  ...

findings.remediation: Call getWindow().setFlags(WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE, WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE) in onCreate() of every Activity that displays sensitive information.

findings.title: Firebase Analytics Enabled Before User Consent
findings.mastgTest: MASTG-TEST-0060 — Testing Whether the App Contains Sensitive Data in Logs
findings.cwe: CWE-359 — Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
findings.description: The AndroidManifest.xml does not set firebase_analytics_collection_enabled to false. On first cold launch, before any consent UI is shown to the user, Firebase Analytics begins collecting device identifiers, session data, and screen events. The SharedPreferences written at first launch confirm analytics are active with consent_source: -10 (no consent recorded).

evidence.title: Manifest analytics flags
evidence.output: (no matching <meta-data> entries — all flags absent, defaulting to true)

findings.remediation: Add <meta-data android:name="firebase_analytics_collection_enabled" android:value="false" /> to AndroidManifest.xml and call FirebaseAnalytics.getInstance(context).setAnalyticsCollectionEnabled(true) only after the user provides explicit consent. Reference: https://firebase.google.com/docs/analytics/configure-data-collection
